nativeRange.habitat: Swampy lowland wetlands, marshes, irrigation ditches, rice paddies, and still to slow-moving tropical freshwater across Asia. The species is native to Asia but is closely related to Ceratopteris thalictroides and C. cornuta — species that are collectively widespread across the tropics — and some taxonomic authorities treat the three as forms of a single pan-tropical complex.

decorPreferences: fine gravel or planted-tank substrate when planted
soft rooting — bury only the base of the rhizome

summary: Water Sprite Lace Leaf (Ceratopteris siliquosa) is a fine-leaved Asian aquatic fern prized for its deeply dissected, lacy fronds. Fast-growing and beginner-friendly, it can be planted in the background or floated at the surface, where it forms a feathery canopy used as cover by fry and shrimp.
careNotes:
The Water Sprite Lace Leaf is a finer-leaved Ceratopteris form, distinguished from the common Water Sprite (Ceratopteris thalictroides) by its more deeply dissected, lacier fronds. Its precise classification is unsettled: some authorities recognise Ceratopteris siliquosa as a distinct Asian species, while others treat it as a synonym of C. cornuta or a form of C. thalictroides. In the aquarium trade it is consistently sold as a separate offering because of its finer foliage texture.

Care follows the same pattern as standard Water Sprite and is generally considered beginner-friendly. It can be grown two ways: planted in fine gravel, sand, or aquasoil as a midground-to-background specimen, or left floating at the surface where it produces a feathery canopy with trailing roots that fry and shrimp use for cover. Moderate-to-bright lighting encourages faster, bushier growth. CO2 supplementation is not required but tends to accelerate the already-fast growth rate and may enhance the fine lacy texture of the fronds. The plant is a heavy water-column feeder and strips nitrate and phosphate quickly, making it particularly useful in new tanks and breeding setups where nutrient stability matters.

Rooting is relatively weak compared to true substrate plants. When planting, bury only the base of the rhizome (the horizontal stem from which the fronds grow) — never the crown itself. Floating is equally viable and requires no substrate at all. Older leaves regularly produce small adventitious plantlets (young plants that sprout along the leaf margins while still attached to the parent) that detach and root on their own; this self-propagating tendency is part of what makes the species easy to maintain.
breedingNotes: Water Sprite Lace Leaf reproduces most readily through adventitious plantlets — small daughter plants that sprout along the margins of mature leaves, developing their own roots while still attached to the parent. Once large enough, they detach naturally or can be gently removed and replanted in substrate or left to float. Lateral shoots from the base of the plant can also be separated and grown on independently. Spore reproduction is possible only on emersed or fertile fronds and does not occur under normal submerged aquarium conditions.

primary.name: Modern Aquarium
primary.notes: Primary source for the 'Lace Leaf' trade form: lighting (moderate, faster/bushier with stronger light), CO2 (not required), growth rate (fast under good conditions), temperature 71–82 °F (~22–28 °C), placement (background planted or floating), substrate (fine gravel/planted-tank substrate; not required if floating), fertilisation (benefits from nutrient-rich environment, draws nutrients from water column), and care level (easy). Native range, family, and detailed pH/hardness/size not specified by this source.

additional.name: Buce Plant
additional.notes: Cross-checked: common names (Water Sprite, Lace Leaf, Indian Fern), origin described as 'cosmopolitan', lighting (low–medium), CO2 (recommended), growth rate (moderate-to-fast), placement (stem plant, floated or planted), adult height 6–15 in (~15–38 cm), pH 6.5–7.5, substrate not critical (weak rooting), foliar nutrient absorption, propagation by cutting along the stem, care level (easy). Note: this source lists the family as 'Ceratophyllaceae', which is incorrect (Ceratophyllaceae is the hornwort family) — Ceratopteris is in Pteridaceae; this entry uses the correct Pteridaceae placement inherited from the well-established C. thalictroides taxonomy.

additional.name: Wikipedia — Ceratopteris (genus)
additional.notes: Background reference on the genus Ceratopteris (family Pteridaceae, order Polypodiales) and the ongoing taxonomic revision of the C. thalictroides / C. cornuta / C. richardii complex. The article does not list C. siliquosa as a currently-accepted species name, supporting the documented synonymy/flux: aquarium trade name 'Lace Leaf' applied to Ceratopteris siliquosa is variably treated as a synonym of C. cornuta or as a form of C. thalictroides depending on authority.
